questions.question_text: How many business days does the attorney review period last in a New Jersey real estate contract?
distractors: Five business days
Seven calendar days
Ten business days
questions.explanation: New Jersey real estate contracts include a distinctive 3-business-day attorney review period. During this time, either party's attorney can review, modify, or void the contract entirely.

questions.question_text: During the attorney review period, the buyer's attorney sends a disapproval letter. What happens to the contract?
questions.correct_answer: The contract is voided and deposit returned
distractors: The seller must accept the attorney's changes
The buyer forfeits the earnest money deposit
The contract remains binding for 30 more days
questions.explanation: If either party's attorney disapproves the contract during the 3-business-day review period, the contract is voided and the deposit must be returned to the buyer. This is a distinctive protection in NJ practice.

questions.question_text: A buyer signs a purchase contract on Friday afternoon. When does the 3-business-day attorney review period end?
questions.correct_answer: At the end of the following Wednesday
distractors: At the end of the following Monday
At midnight the following Sunday
At the end of the following Friday
questions.explanation: The 3-business-day period counts only business days, excluding weekends. Starting from Friday, the three business days are Monday, Tuesday, and Wednesday, so the review period ends Wednesday.

questions.question_text: What power does an attorney have during the NJ attorney review period beyond simply approving the contract?
questions.correct_answer: The attorney may modify or void the contract
distractors: The attorney can only suggest non-binding edits
The attorney may extend the review to 10 days
The attorney can renegotiate the purchase price only
questions.explanation: During the 3-business-day review period, either party's attorney can approve, modify, or completely void the contract. This broad power makes the review period a significant feature of NJ real estate practice.

questions.question_text: A seller's attorney sends modifications during the review period that the buyer rejects. What is the status of the original contract?
questions.correct_answer: The original contract is effectively voided
distractors: The original contract remains fully binding
The seller must accept the buyer's position
A neutral mediator must resolve the dispute
questions.explanation: When an attorney sends modifications during the review period, it functions as a disapproval of the original terms. If the other party rejects those modifications, the contract is voided and negotiations may start fresh.

questions.question_text: In New Jersey, a listing agreement must be in what form to be enforceable?
distractors: Verbal agreement with a witness present
Notarized oral agreement
Either written or oral form
questions.explanation: New Jersey requires listing agreements to be in writing to be enforceable. This protects both the broker and the seller by clearly documenting the terms of the agency relationship.

questions.question_text: A NJ listing agreement fails to include an expiration date. What is the effect on the agreement?
distractors: It automatically expires after 12 months
It remains valid until the property sells
NJREC assigns a default 90-day term
questions.explanation: NJ listing agreements must include specific provisions including an expiration date. Missing required elements like the expiration date can render the agreement unenforceable and may violate NJREC regulations.

questions.question_text: Under which listing type does the broker earn a commission regardless of who procures the buyer?
distractors: Exclusive agency listing only
Open listing agreement
Net listing agreement
questions.explanation: With an exclusive right to sell listing, the broker earns the commission no matter who finds the buyer, including the seller. This provides the greatest protection for the broker and is the most common listing type.

questions.question_text: A NJ seller signs an exclusive agency listing. The seller finds a buyer independently without the broker's help. Does the broker earn a commission?
questions.correct_answer: No, the seller may sell independently commission-free
distractors: Yes, all exclusive listings guarantee a commission
Yes, but at a reduced rate of 50 percent
No, and the listing is automatically terminated
questions.explanation: An exclusive agency listing allows the seller to find a buyer independently without owing a commission. However, if any other broker procures the buyer, the listing broker earns the commission.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is a required element in a NJ listing agreement per NJREC regulations?
distractors: A guaranteed minimum sale price
The buyer's pre-approval letter
The seller's mortgage payoff amount
questions.explanation: NJREC requires listing agreements to include property description, listing price, commission terms, expiration date, and broker's authority. These provisions protect both parties and ensure clear expectations.

questions.question_text: Under the Statute of Frauds, what form must a real estate purchase agreement take to be enforceable in NJ?
questions.correct_answer: It must be in writing and signed
distractors: An oral agreement with two witnesses suffices
A verbal agreement recorded on video is valid
Email confirmation alone satisfies the requirement
questions.explanation: The Statute of Frauds requires real estate contracts to be in writing and signed by the parties to be enforceable. This applies to purchase agreements, leases over a certain duration, and other real property conveyances.

questions.question_text: A buyer submits an offer on a NJ property. The seller responds with different price terms. What has occurred legally?
questions.correct_answer: The counteroffer terminates the original offer
distractors: Both offers remain open for acceptance
The seller's response is merely a suggestion
The original offer remains valid for 48 hours
questions.explanation: A counteroffer legally terminates the original offer. The buyer cannot later accept the original terms once the seller has made a counteroffer, as that original offer no longer exists.

questions.question_text: A seller instructs the listing agent not to present offers below $400,000. A $380,000 offer arrives. What should the agent do?
questions.correct_answer: Present it unless written instructions say otherwise
distractors: Reject it immediately on behalf of the seller
Hold it until a higher offer comes in
Submit it only if the buyer increases to $400,000
questions.explanation: NJ agents must present ALL offers to the seller unless the seller has provided written instructions to the contrary. Verbal instructions alone are insufficient to relieve the agent of this presentation duty.

questions.question_text: A NJ purchase contract includes a time-is-of-the-essence clause. The buyer misses the closing date by one day. What is the likely result?
questions.correct_answer: The seller may declare the buyer in default
distractors: The closing automatically extends by 30 days
The clause is unenforceable in New Jersey
The buyer receives a 10-day grace period
questions.explanation: Time-is-of-the-essence clauses are significant in NJ. When included, deadlines are strictly enforced and missing even a single day can constitute a breach, allowing the other party to declare default.

questions.question_text: A buyer's agent receives three competing offers on the same NJ property. What is the agent's obligation?
questions.correct_answer: Present all offers to the seller promptly
distractors: Present only the highest offer received
Hold all offers until the listing expires
Select the strongest offer for presentation
questions.explanation: NJ agents have a duty to present all offers to the seller unless instructed otherwise in writing. The agent cannot screen, filter, or prioritize offers on their own and must present each one promptly.
